The Latin Alcuin reads the names mystically — Bethsaida "house of hunters," fishers of souls; Galilee a place of "transition" — and "Follow me" as a call to imitate Christ's humility and suffering.

The Reformed Calvin makes this the text for the power of the gospel word: Philip was "inflamed by this single word," yet the same word strikes many ears in vain; the external preaching is unfruitful "except the secret grace of God quicken it," for which we ought to pray. The Puritan Matthew Henry adds the note of preventing grace — "Jesus findeth Philip"; Christ "sought us, and found us, before we made any enquiries after him."

The grammatical-historical voices harmonise the chronology: Barnes carefully distinguishes this acquaintance-and-conviction from the later formal call to apostleship at the sea (Matthew 4:18), so that John and the Synoptics do not conflict; Robertson and Ellicott note that "Follow me" is the verb by which Jesus repeatedly wins disciples.
44:
The note that "Philip was of Bethsaida, the city of Andrew and Peter" draws brief comment. The grammatical-historical voices treat it as one of the eye-witness touches of local knowledge: this is Bethsaida of Galilee (Ellicott, Robertson, cf. John 12:21), distinct from Bethsaida Julias east of the lake; and it explains the chain of calling, Philip following the example of his townsmen Andrew and Peter.

The Reformed Calvin alone draws doctrine from the place: its very name magnifies the grace shown, for Christ elsewhere curses Bethsaida (Matthew 11:21); to draw apostles from so ungodly a town was "as if they had been brought out of the lowest hell." The Reformed Jamieson-Fausset-Brown add only Bengel's terse aside that the work was "not done without dust on man's part" — the human means by which grace travels from neighbour to neighbour.
45:
Philip finds Nathanael with the news, "We have found him of whom Moses in the law and the prophets did write, Jesus of Nazareth, the son of Joseph." The witnesses agree on the substance — that Philip announces the fulfilment of Deuteronomy 18:18, Genesis 49:10, and the prophets — and most identify Nathanael with Bartholomew (Clarke, Wesley, Robertson, Gill, Ellicott), since the Synoptics pair Bartholomew with Philip while John never names him. The grammatical-historical voices note Philip's diligent searching of the Scriptures and read "son of Joseph... of Nazareth" as the common supposition of the day; Ellicott prizes the evangelist's "dramatic accuracy" in giving the words exactly as spoken, without correcting them by his own knowledge of the birth at Bethlehem, and Robertson speaks of "the irony of St. John."

The Reformed Calvin makes the verse a lesson on faulty-but-sincere witness: Philip errs twice — calling Christ "son of Joseph" and a native of Nazareth — yet because his aim is to lead his brother to the true Christ born at Bethlehem (Matthew 2:1), God approves and crowns his diligence. Better, Calvin urges, to "stammer ridiculously" and hold the true Christ than, with eloquence, to introduce a false one — a thrust at the Papists. The Wesleyan Wesley likewise notes that Philip thought him of Nazareth, "not knowing he was born in Bethlehem."
46:
Nathanael's "Can any good thing come out of Nazareth?" and Philip's "Come and see" draw a shared reading: prejudice met by invitation to examine. The grammatical-historical voices explain the scorn — Nazareth's poor repute, perhaps sharpened by town-rivalry, Cana being near (Robertson, Ellicott) — and note the parallel slur, "out of Galilee ariseth no prophet" (John 7:52). Clarke, following Pearce, suggests Nathanael may rather mean that the Messiah, the promised "good thing," was looked for from Bethlehem, not Nazareth (Jeremiah 33:15). Barnes turns it into a maxim against pre-judging: do not condemn by prejudice but "come and see," and the foolish objections vanish.

The Reformed Calvin diagnoses a twofold error: Nathanael too readily believes Philip's misstatement and is swayed by contempt of the place — both being Satan's stratagems to keep men from Christ, whether by falsehood or by the offence of his lowliness. Yet Calvin commends Nathanael's readiness to let his prejudice be corrected by inquiry, and rejects the reading that takes the words as an affirmation. The Wesleyan Wesley draws the same lesson: guard against popular prejudice, which led even an honest heart to suspect Christ, until "his integrity prevailed... and laid him open to the force of evidence."
47:
"Behold an Israelite indeed, in whom is no guile" sets the witnesses on the figure of Jacob/Israel. With broad agreement they hear an allusion to the patriarch: the grammatical-historical voices note that the word for "guile" (dolos) is the very term the Greek Old Testament uses of the subtlety by which Jacob took the blessing (Genesis 27:35), so that Nathanael is an Israel without the supplanter's guile, like the Servant in whose mouth was no deceit (Isaiah 53:9). Lightfoot pictures him under the fig tree in private prayer — "an Israelite indeed" precisely because, unlike the street-corner hypocrites, he sought retirement to pray; Ellicott presses the parallel of Jacob wrestling alone and prevailing with God (Genesis 32:24).

The Latin Augustine guards the praise from misunderstanding: "no guile" does not mean "no sin," for none is born without need of the Physician; guile is to say one thing and think another, and Nathanael lacks it because, a sinner, he confesses his sin rather than feigning righteousness (Psalm 32:2). The Reformed Calvin makes the verse a definition of true Christianity — integrity of heart before God and uprightness before men, set against the hypocrisy that names itself Israel; for "they are not all Israel who are of Israel" (Romans 9:6), and Christ here forewarns of the nation's coming unbelief. The Antiochene Chrysostom adds that Nathanael's very hesitation showed him guileless — speaking "nothing to gain favour, or gratify malice" — and more deeply read in the prophets than Philip.
48:
"Whence knowest thou me? ... Before that Philip called thee, when thou wast under the fig tree, I saw thee." The witnesses are united that Christ here displays a divine, heart-searching sight. The grammatical-historical voices fill in the setting: the fig tree was a customary place of private prayer and meditation, Jews retiring under trees for devotion (Barnes, Clarke); Jesus saw not merely Nathanael's body but his secret heart and worship, "I saw thee" meaning his thoughts and desires were open (Robertson). Barnes draws the lessons that Christ sees what is done in secret, marks our private devotions, and "judges of our character chiefly by our private devotions" (Psalm 139:2); Ellicott pictures the fig tree of Nathanael's own garden, the place of unseen prayer (Micah 4:4, Zechariah 3:10).

The Reformed Calvin presses the inference to its point: both to know hearts and to see what is hidden from the eye belong to God alone, and from things of the same class Nathanael rightly concludes that Christ does not speak "as a man." The useful doctrine, Calvin adds, is that "when we are not thinking of Christ, we are observed by him" — a watchfulness meant to bring us back when we wander.
49:
Nathanael's confession — "Rabbi, thou art the Son of God; thou art the King of Israel" — opens a genuine debate over its depth. The Antiochene Chrysostom raises it pointedly: Nathanael and Peter (Matthew 16:16) speak the same words but not in the same sense. Peter confessed "Son of God" as very God, and was called blessed; Nathanael, Chrysostom argues, meant it of a mere man, for he at once narrows it to "King of Israel," whereas the Son of God is King of all the world — and so he is led on to greater things. The Reformed Calvin reads the confession as real but limited: faith must fix not on Christ's essence only but on his office and saving power as King; yet Nathanael's "King of Israel" is still confined within narrow bounds, not yet the world-wide kingdom we, to whom its extent is revealed, must confess.

The grammatical-historical voices resist toning the confession down. Robertson holds there is "no reason to tone down the noble confession," both being Messianic titles drawn from Psalm 2:7, and "King of Israel" reappearing at the Triumphal Entry (John 12:13); Barnes reasons that Nathanael, finding his heart read, concluded Christ must be more than man. Wesley reads it plainly as a confession of "both the person and office of Christ." Thus the schools divide: the Greek father and the Reformer hear a true but incipient faith to be enlarged, while the grammarians hear a full and noble acknowledgment.
50:
"Because I said... I saw thee under the fig tree, believest thou? thou shalt see greater things than these." The comment is brief and harmonious. The grammatical-historical voices note that "believest thou?" may be read as a question or, better, as an assertion — "on this evidence thou believest" (Ellicott, comparing John 20:29) — and that the "greater things" are the fuller proofs of his Messiahship promised in v.51 (Barnes, Clarke). Clarke draws the encouragement that Nathanael's faith, resting now on a single sign, shall not stop there but see "more numerous and express proofs."

The Reformed Calvin reads it not as a reproof of over-easy belief but as Christ's approval of a ready faith, which he promises to confirm "by stronger arguments"; and he marks the shift from the private token given to one man toward a proof that would be "common to all" — Christ breaking off, as it were, from addressing Nathanael alone to address them all.
51:
Christ's solemn promise — "Verily, verily... ye shall see heaven open, and the angels of God ascending and descending upon the Son of man" — finds the witnesses unanimous on its root: this is the fulfilment of Jacob's ladder (Genesis 28:12), with Christ himself the bond between heaven and earth. The grammatical-historical voices note the doubled "Amen," found in the New Testament only on Christ's lips in this Gospel — he is himself "the Amen, the faithful witness" (Revelation 3:14; so Lightfoot, from Isaiah 65:16) — and the plural "ye shall see," addressed beyond Nathanael to all the disciples. The perfect participle pictures heaven "standing open," the symbol of free intercourse between God and man (Isaiah 64:1), later realised in Stephen's vision (Acts 7:56). Robertson and Ellicott press the title "Son of man": over against Nathanael's Jewish titles, it names Christ as the true representative of the race — homo, not vir — the second Adam in whom heaven and earth meet. Clarke reads the angels as couriers between the prince and his ambassador, the whole work of reconciliation now passing through the Son of man (2 Corinthians 5:19).

The Reformed Calvin warns against fixing the promise on one time or place: it points to "something perpetual," for the kingdom of God, once closed against us, is now opened in Christ; the angels ascend and descend as ministers of God's kindness to the whole Church, for Christ's honour — shown visibly to Stephen (Acts 7:55), on the mount (Matthew 17:5), and at the Ascension (Acts 1:9). The Wesleyan Wesley, with Barnes, leans rather to particular future events — "ye shall see me come in my glory with all the angels," or the ministrations of angels at the resurrection and ascension. So the schools agree on Jacob's ladder and the Son of man, and divide only on whether the "hereafter" is a perpetual mediatorial state (Calvin, Ellicott) or a series of coming events (Wesley, Barnes). The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem touches the verse only to gather from the doubled "verily" the promise of life eternal by faith.
